topic: A person discovers their mentor has retired without saying goodbye
raw_output:
[story 1]

The silence in the office was the loudest thing I had ever heard. It pressed in on me, thick and suffocating, like wading through cold syrup. I kept glancing at the doorway, half-expecting to see him materialize, perhaps with a well-timed, apologetic smile. But the door remained closed. My hand, hovering over the keyboard, felt clumsy and heavy. A strange, hollow ache settled low in my chest, a physical tightening that made it hard to draw a full breath. I reread the last email, the one confirming his departure, the words stark and final. I stood up slowly, the chair scraping faintly against the polished floor, and walked to the window. The city lights blurred, mocking the sudden, sharp emptiness where his presence used to anchor my days.

[story 2]

"So, that's it?" The question escaped my lips, thin and reedy, sounding alien even to my own ears. I gripped the worn leather of my portfolio so tightly my knuckles turned white. Every sound in the room—the hum of the ventilation, the distant traffic—seemed amplified, grating against my nerves. I kept expecting a sound, a familiar cadence of his voice offering some gentle explanation, some reassurance that this wasn't the end of *us*. But there was only the quiet, the undeniable fact of his absence. A prickle started at the base of my neck, a cold, unsettling sensation that made the fine hairs stand up. I took a shaky step back, needing space, needing to process the abrupt severing of a long-held connection.

[story 3]

I stared at the framed photograph on his desk—a picture taken during a particularly chaotic project, his eyes alight with a kind of boundless energy. It felt like an accusation. I didn't move for a long time, just letting the weight of the news settle over me, heavy as a wet blanket. My throat felt tight, constricted, as if something vital had been squeezed out of me. When I finally forced myself to speak, my voice emerged surprisingly flat. "Did... did he say anything?" The query was barely audible, a whisper against the vast, empty expanse of the room. I felt a strange tremor run through my hands, a restless energy that refused to settle, demanding an answer that simply wasn't coming.
